title: OASA Telematics Redesign
engagement: National and Kapodistrian University of Athens
summary: For my MSc dissertation, I redesigned the OASA Telematics Android app. I researched how passengers use arrivals and vehicle locations, rebuilt those tasks in Figma, and tested the prototype with users and UX experts.
problem: At a stop, passengers may need arrivals, vehicle positions, route details, and service notices at the same time. In the original app, those pieces sat in separate views. I redesigned the main tasks so someone could choose a route and keep checking it without restarting the search.

cover.alt: OASA Telematics mobile app redesign shown on a phone beside the project title
cover.caption: OASA Telematics mobile app redesign

context.heading: Dissertation scope
context.body: This was an independent MSc project at the National and Kapodistrian University of Athens. It was not commissioned by OASA. I designed the Android prototype for daily and occasional passengers, including older people and people who use accessibility settings. It follows the main journey through account access, planning, tracking, tickets, and support.

research.heading: What passengers checked first
research.body: In the initial survey, arrivals were the most-used feature in the existing app. Vehicle positions came next. Respondents asked for offline access, better live tracking, clearer maps, and service notices that were harder to miss. Six focus-group participants also wanted nearby stops, recent trips, and fewer competing elements on each screen.

stats.label: people responded to the initial transport-app survey
wanted an offline mode for essential journey information
asked for better live bus and trolley tracking

structure.heading: Organising the app around five tasks
structure.body: Fifty-two percent of survey participants thought the existing app already had the right number of functions. The problem was how those functions were grouped. Scenarios, hierarchical task analysis, essential use cases, and a closed card sort with ten participants produced five categories: trip planning, live vehicle location, route search, tickets, and profile settings. I drew the first flows in Whimsical, then built and linked the detailed screens in Figma.

boards.alt: Figma canvas showing authentication and route-planning screens for OASA Telematics
boards.caption: Authentication and journey-planning flows

boards.alt: Figma canvas showing ticket, profile, settings, and support screens for OASA Telematics
boards.caption: Tickets, profile, settings, and support

groups.heading: Account access and registration
groups.body: The welcome screen offers sign-in, registration, and guest access. Guest access was added after testing because an account should not block someone who only needs the next arrival. Registration asks for account details first and treats the profile photo as optional before opening the planner.

groups.heading: Planning and tracking a trip
groups.body: I kept the map behind each planning state so the passenger does not lose their location while choosing a destination or comparing departures. Selecting a route opens the walking and boarding steps. Once the route is active, the passenger can check nearby vehicles, the current vehicle position, the next stop, and any service notification without setting it up again.

groups.heading: ATH.ENA Card and fare information
groups.body: ATH.ENA Card information sits in the main navigation beside routes. The available Figma export shows the overview state, where a passenger can check the card and open fare information without searching through settings.

screens.alt: Tickets overview with ATH.ENA Card information and fare options
screens.caption: Open the ATH.ENA Card overview

groups.heading: Profile, favourites, and recent routes
groups.body: Favourite routes and recent journeys sit in the profile with the passenger's account details. They support repeat trips without filling the planner with personal history. A separate edit screen handles the profile photo, username, name, and phone number.

screens.alt: Profile overview with personal information, favourite routes, and recent journeys
screens.caption: Review favourites and history

screens.alt: Profile editing screen for photo, username, full name, and phone number
screens.caption: Edit personal details

groups.heading: Settings, accessibility, and support
groups.body: I separated device permissions from accessibility controls. One decides which data the app can use. The other changes text size, contrast, screen-reader support, text to speech, and external-device support. The same settings index links to route notifications, service news, contact, privacy, and app information.

screens.alt: Settings overview with permissions, accessibility, route notifications, privacy, news, and app information

evaluation.heading: What changed after testing
evaluation.body: Forty participants completed the WAMMI questionnaire after using the Figma prototype. I also ran five think-aloud sessions and a heuristic review with five experts. The WAMMI score was 91.17%. It measures perceived usability of this prototype, not a released app. Testing also exposed unclear time controls, weak departure and arrival labels, hard-to-find nearby stops, and an account gate that slowed down first use.

stats.label: global WAMMI usability score for the prototype
experts reviewed the prototype against usability heuristics

changes: Added guest access and moved the origin-destination swap control beside the fields.
Labelled alternate departure time instead of relying on an unfamiliar icon.
Made the current stop and nearby stops visible in the journey context.
Grouped notifications and gave unread service updates a clearer state.
Renamed saved routes as favourites and replaced the ambiguous icon with a heart.
Added voice-notification options and a direct route to support.

output.heading: Prototype coverage
output.body: The final Android prototype includes every distinct state in the supplied Figma exports: onboarding, registration, trip planning, live vehicle and stop tracking, service notifications, tickets, profile editing, permissions, accessibility controls, and support.
